topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom felt thick and suffocating, heavy with forced merriment and the cloying scent of expensive perfume. I watched from the periphery, nursing a lukewarm glass of champagne, my jaw tight. Then, there he was. Standing near the punch bowl, looking annoyingly well-adjusted in a tailored suit, was Mark. He was laughing with someone—a woman whose smile seemed entirely too bright for the occasion. My shoulders tensed almost imperceptibly. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up. I took a deliberate, slow sip of my drink, focusing intently on the condensation dripping down the glass, refusing to let my gaze linger on him. When our eyes briefly met across the crowded room, I gave the barest twitch of my lips—a movement so small it was almost missed—before turning sharply toward the exit, needing air that didn't smell like manufactured happiness.

[story 2]

I’d rehearsed my composure for weeks, practicing the neutral expression in the mirror, but the moment I saw him, the carefully constructed wall I’d built around my emotions began to crumble. He was standing by the buffet, looking exactly as polished and untouchable as I remembered, only now framed by the warm glow of the reception lights. A sudden, sharp heat prickled the back of my neck. I felt a distinct urge to physically recoil, to shrink away from the proximity of him. I forced myself to move, pretending to be deeply engrossed in a conversation with a distant acquaintance. When he finally turned his head in my direction, I met his gaze for a fraction of a second too long. My expression was carefully blank, perhaps a little too cold around the edges, and I felt a distinct tightening in my chest, a sensation like being constricted by an invisible wire.

[story 3]

The noise of the celebration was overwhelming, a relentless wave of cheerful chatter and booming music. I found myself leaning against a marble pillar, trying to merge into the architectural detail, anything to avoid the central thoroughfare where I knew he would be. And there he was, looking effortlessly charming, holding court near the dance floor. A slow, unpleasant tightening began in my stomach. I watched him interact, cataloging every casual gesture, every easy smile, and it felt like watching a performance I was entirely excluded from. When our paths inadvertently crossed near the bar, I deliberately shifted my weight, creating a slight barrier between us. My voice, when I offered a brief, clipped greeting to someone else nearby, was notably drier than intended, a deliberate lack of warmth aimed squarely at the atmosphere surrounding him.
